logline: Every artifact in this story is real — this is a dramatized reenactment of one of history's most haunting underwater discoveries.

auto_replenish.title_style: a 5-10 word plain-case English sentence stating the discovery like a news hook (e.g. 'Divers Found a 2,000-Year-Old Computer'); no quotes, no emoji, no ALL-CAPS words, no colons.
auto_replenish.brief: Each episode dramatizes ONE REAL underwater archaeological discovery as found-footage from a dive team's helmet camera. FIXED 5-SHOT ARC: (1) descent into dark water toward the site; (2) approach and entry — the site emerges from the murk and the team slips inside; (3) one eerie beat (statues staring, a sudden animal, impossible stillness); (4) THE FIND — torchlight lands on the artifact/subject (hook_shot=4); (5) recovery/documentation and ascent — gloved hands, lift bags or sample cases (if the site is protected, the team documents and takes NOTHING), then surface glow above, site fading below, one last haunting look. NARRATION: calm documentary-horror gravitas, past tense, built ONLY from the facts of the chosen FACT BANK entry — NEVER invent names, dates, objects or numbers; end with a line that lingers (what remains below, what we still cannot explain). TITLE_CARD: title = the artifact/site name (max 40 chars); subtitle = 'Region, Country — found YEAR' (use 'flooded YEAR' / 'first mapped YEAR' when that fits the entry better). NEVER show human remains, skeletons or bodies on screen. FACT BANK — each episode uses EXACTLY ONE entry that no existing episode has used yet (check existing titles/synopses; always put the entry's name inside the synopsis): [1] Mary Rose — Solent, England — located 1971, raised 1982; Henry VIII's flagship, sank 1545; thousands of artifacts including hundreds of longbows. [2] Vasa — Stockholm harbor, Sweden — found 1956 by Anders Franzén, raised 1961 nearly intact; the great warship sank on her maiden voyage in 1628. [3] Riace Bronzes — off Riace, Calabria, Italy — found 1972 by a snorkeler at ~8 m; two full-size ancient Greek bronze warriors, c. 460-450 BC. [4] Kyrenia Ship — off Kyrenia, Cyprus — found 1965 by sponge diver Andreas Cariolou; 4th-century-BC Greek merchant ship, hull raised and displayed in Kyrenia Castle. [5] Cape Gelidonya wreck — off Antalya, Türkiye — excavated 1960 by George Bass; Late Bronze Age (~1200 BC) copper-ingot cargo; the first scientific underwater excavation in history. [6] Serçe Limanı Glass Wreck — near Marmaris, Türkiye — found 1973; 11th-century ship carrying three tons of Islamic glass; now in Bodrum. [7] Sunken Port Royal — Jamaica — two-thirds of the pirate city sank in the 1692 earthquake; excavations 1981-1990 recovered a pocket watch stopped near 11:43. [8] Cleopatra's royal quarter on Antirhodos — Alexandria harbor, Egypt — mapped from 1996 by Franck Goddio; sphinxes and priest statues found on the drowned island. [9] Pavlopetri — off Laconia, Greece — mapped 1968 by Nicholas Flemming; the world's oldest submerged town, Bronze Age streets and tombs at ~4 m. [10] Atlit-Yam — off Atlit, Israel — found 1984; a 9,000-year-old Neolithic village 8-12 m down, with a stone circle around a spring and the oldest known wells. [11] Belitung wreck — Java Sea, Indonesia — found 1998 by sea-cucumber divers; 9th-century Arabian dhow carrying ~60,000 Tang-dynasty ceramics and gold. [12] San José galleon — off Cartagena, Colombia — located 2015 by a deep-water robot at ~600 m; Spanish treasure galleon sunk in battle in 1708; its gold is still on the seabed. [13] Dokos wreck — off Dokos island, Greece — found 1975 by Peter Throckmorton; the oldest known shipwreck cargo on Earth, c. 2200 BC. [14] Nemi ships — Lake Nemi, Italy — Caligula's two giant pleasure barges recovered 1929-1932 by partly draining the sacred lake; both burned in 1944, bronze fittings survive. [15] Lake Titicaca offerings — Khoa Reef near the Island of the Sun, Bolivia — excavations 2013-2019 raised Inca and Tiwanaku stone offering boxes holding gold figurines and spondylus shell. [16] Grand Congloué — near Marseille, France — worked from 1952 by Jacques Cousteau's team; Roman-era wine-ship cargo of thousands of amphorae. [17] Yonaguni Monument — off Yonaguni, Japan — found 1986 by diver Kihachiro Aratake; colossal stepped stone terraces ~25 m down; scientists still dispute whether nature or human hands cut them (present honestly as an open mystery). [18] Bajo de la Campana — off Murcia, Spain — excavated 2007-2011; 7th-century-BC Phoenician ship carrying elephant tusks and raw ivory. [19] Mahdia wreck — off Mahdia, Tunisia — found 1907 by Greek sponge divers; Roman ship packed with Greek bronzes and sixty marble columns. [20] Mazotos wreck — off Mazotos, Cyprus — found 2006; 4th-century-BC merchantman with hundreds of wine amphorae still stacked as loaded. [21] Sinop D — Black Sea, off Sinop, Türkiye — found 2000 by Robert Ballard's team at ~320 m; a ~1,600-year-old ship so preserved in the oxygen-free dark that its mast still stands. [22] Rungholt — Wadden Sea, Germany — the trading town drowned in the great storm flood of 1362; in 2023 researchers located its lost church under the mudflats; legend says its bells still ring in storms. IF every FACT BANK entry has been used, choose another world-famous, thoroughly documented underwater discovery you are absolutely CERTAIN about (real name, real place, real year) and follow the same rules.
